en.extract:
The shy heathwren (Hylacola cauta) is a species of small bird in the family Acanthizidae, endemic to Australia. They inhabit mostly mallee woodland that has relatively dense shrub and heath understorey. 


Taxonomy
Their taxonomic name was formerly Calamanthus cautus—classing them as fieldwrens—until they were renamed in 2008. The generic name Hylacola derives from the Greek hylē 'woodland' and the Latin -cola 'dweller'. The specific epithet derives from the Latin cautus 'shy, wary'. Common names for the species include shy hylacola and mallee heathwren. Four subspecies have been recognised: the nominate subspecies Hylacola cauta cauta in South Australia and Victoria; H. c. macrorhynchus in New South Wales; H. c. halmaturina on Kangaroo Island; and H. c. whitlocki in Western Australia. 


Description
The birds are cock-tailed with a chestnut rump that darkens towards the tip. Their feathers are white with brown streaking underneath and greyish-brown on the back and crown. They have a white eyebrow, tail tip and patch on the flight feathers, and a black bill. Their eyes are brown to yellowish-brown and legs are slate-brown. Females have slightly duller colouring, and immature birds are duller again with some of these being fawn-coloured underneath. They have a total length when adults of 12–14 cm (4.7–5.5 in).


Distribution and threats
They are uncommon residents across a wide part of southern Australia. Ranging from near West Wyalong in New South Wales to the Murchison River in Western Australia. In New South Wales, they are found in two isolated populations: one between Leeton, Willandra National Park, Nymagee and West Wyalong; and the other from Balranald to Trentham Cliffs. Within the state they are seen as a threatened and vulnerable, largely due to human-wrought habitat loss, and predation by foxes and cats.


Behaviour


Diet
Shy heathwrens feed mostly on ground-dwelling insects, and rarely on seeds. 


Nesting
Their ground-level nests are dome-shaped and usually concealed within grass tussocks or shrubs. They typically lay 2 or 3 freckled and pinkish eggs.


References

They inhabit mostly [[Mallee (habit)|mallee]] woodland that has relatively dense shrub and [[Heath (habitat)|heath]] understorey. ==Taxonomy== Their taxonomic name was formerly ''Calamanthus cautus''—classing them as fieldwrens—until they were renamed in 2008.{{cite book|last=Christidis|first=L|author2=Boles, W.E |title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|location=Collingwood, Victoria|year=2008|isbn=978-0-643-06511-6}} The generic name ''Hylacola'' derives from the Greek ''hylē'' 'woodland' and the Latin ''-cola'' 'dweller'.{{cite web|url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird-names|last=Jobling|first=James A.|access-date = 2020-04-18}} The specific epithet derives from the Latin ''cautus'' 'shy, wary'. Common names for the species include shy hylacola and mallee heathwren.Pizzey, Graham; Doyle, Roy (1980) ''A Field Guide to the Birds of Australia''. Collins Publishers, Sydney. {{ISBN|073222436-5}} Four subspecies have been recognised: the nominate subspecies ''Hylacola cauta cauta'' in South Australia and Victoria; ''H. c. macrorhynchus'' in New South Wales; ''H. c. halmaturina'' on [[Kangaroo Island]]; and ''H. c. whitlocki'' in Western Australia.Morcombe, Michael (2012) ''Field Guide to Australian Birds''. Pascal Press, Glebe, NSW. Revised edition. {{ISBN|978174021417-9}}{{cite taxon|BOW|author=Gregory, P. |year=2020|url= https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/shyhea1/1.0/introduction|title=Shy Heathwren (''Hylacola cauta'')|version=1.0|doi=10.2173/bow.shyhea1.01}} ==Description== The birds are cock-tailed with a chestnut rump that darkens towards the tip. Their feathers are white with brown streaking underneath and greyish-brown on the back and crown. They have a white eyebrow, tail tip and patch on the [[flight feather]]s, and a black [[Beak|bill]]. Their eyes are brown to yellowish-brown and legs are slate-brown. Females have slightly duller colouring, and immature birds are duller again with some of these being fawn-coloured underneath. They have a total length when adults of {{cvt|12|-|14|cm|in}}. ==Distribution and threats== They are uncommon residents across a wide part of southern Australia. Ranging from near [[West Wyalong, New South Wales|West Wyalong]] in [[New South Wales]] to the [[Murchison River (Western Australia)|Murchison River]] in [[Western Australia]]. In New South Wales, they are found in two isolated populations: one between [[Leeton, New South Wales|Leeton]], [[Willandra National Park]], [[Nymagee, New South Wales|Nymagee]] and West Wyalong; and the other from [[Balranald, New South Wales|Balranald]] to [[Trentham Cliffs, New South Wales|Trentham Cliffs]]. Within the state they are seen as a [[threatened]] and [[vulnerable species|vulnerable]], largely due to human-wrought [[Habitat destruction|habitat loss]], and [[predation]] by foxes and cats. ==Behaviour== ===Diet=== Shy heathwrens feed mostly on ground-dwelling insects, and rarely on seeds. ===Nesting=== Their ground-level nests are dome-shaped and usually concealed within grass [[Bunch grass|tussocks]] or shrubs.{{cite web|url=http://www.threatenedspecies.environment.nsw.gov.au/tsprofile/profile.aspx?id=10125|title=Shy Heathwren – Profile|publisher=Department of Environment and Climate Change, NSW|access-date=2008-06-05}} They typically lay 2 or 3 freckled and pinkish eggs.{{cite book|last=Slater|first=Peter|author2=Slater, Pat |author3=Slater, Raoul |title=[[The Slater Field Guide to Australian Birds]]|publisher=Landsdowne|location=Sydney|year=1993|edition=Revised|page=258|isbn=0-947116-99-0}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Commonscat|Calamanthus cautus}} {{Wikispecies|Hylacola cauta}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1311532|from2=Q27075267}} [[Category:Hylacola]] [[Category:Birds of Western Australia]] [[Category:Birds of South Australia]] [[Category:Birds of New South Wales]] [[Category:Birds of Victoria (state)]] [[Category:Endemic birds of Australia]] [[Category:Birds described in 1843]] [[Category:Taxonomy articles created by Polbot]] [[Category:Taxa named by John Gould]]
